name: Laguna Salina Grande from Villarrín de Campos – circular through Reserva Natural de las Lagunas de Villafáfila
name_original: Laguna Salina Grande desde Villarrín de Campos – circular por Reserva Natural de las Lagunas de Villafáfila
region_intro: Hiking around Villafáfila offers access to the unique natural landscape of the Lagunas de Villafáfila Nature Reserve, located in the northeast of Zamora, Spain. This region is characterized by extensive wetlands, including saline lagoons, set within a flat cereal steppe landscape. The gentle topography provides accessible terrain for various outdoor activities, with minimal elevation changes across its routes. Best hiking trails around Villafáfila The most popular hiking route is Laguna Salina Grande — return via the Villafáfila Lagoons Natural Reserve, a 6.3 miles (10.2 km) trail that takes 2 hours 34 minutes to complete. This route offers views of the reserve's largest lagoon and its diverse birdlife. Another top favourite among local hikers is Cistercian Monastery of Santa Maria from Granja de Moruela, an easy 4.8 miles (7.8 km) path. This trail leads to historical ruins, often frequented by white storks. Local hikers also love the Camino Sanabrés and Moreruela Monastery Loop, a 5.0 miles (8.0 km) trail leading through historical sites and the Esla River valley, often completed in about 2 hours 3 minutes. Hiking around Villafáfila is defined by its saline lagoons, vast steppe landscapes, and historical landmarks. The network of routes offers options for different ability levels, from easy strolls to more challenging hikes. The routes in Villafáfila are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.4 stars from 5 reviews. More than 20 hikers have used komoot to explore Villafáfila's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of landscape can I expect when hiking in Villafáfila?
A: Villafáfila is characterized by the unique natural landscape of the Lagunas de Villafáfila Nature Reserve. You'll find extensive wetlands, including saline lagoons like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5438056" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salina Grande Lagoon&lt;/a&gt;, set within a vast, flat cereal steppe. The topography is generally gentle, offering open horizons and accessible terrain.

Q: Is Villafáfila a good destination for birdwatching while hiking?
A: Absolutely. The Lagunas de Villafáfila Nature Reserve is a renowned birdwatcher's paradise. It's home to the highest density of Great Bustards globally and serves as a critical wintering ground for thousands of geese, ducks, and various waders. You can also spot species like the lesser kestrel, Montagu's harrier, and white storks. Several bird observatories, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5438069" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Otero de Sariegos Bird Observatory&lt;/a&gt;, are available along the trails.

Q: Are there any historical sites or landmarks to see on the trails?
A: Yes, several trails incorporate historical sites. For instance, you can explore routes that lead to the ruins of the 12th-century Cistercian Monastery of Santa María de Moreruela. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025267178/cistercian-monastery-of-santa-maria-from-granja-de-moreruela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cistercian Monastery of Santa Maria from Granja de Moruela&lt;/a&gt; trail is an easy option to visit these ruins, which are often frequented by white storks. You might also encounter an ancient Roman bridge near the lagoons.

Q: What is the best time of year to hike in Villafáfila?
A: The best time depends on your interests. Winter is ideal for observing large numbers of geese and ducks, while spring is excellent for waders. The lagoons' water levels fluctuate seasonally, potentially drying out in summer, which can affect bird sightings. The Visitor Center (Casa del Parque) can provide current information on water levels.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Villafáfila?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes available. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025222045/laguna-salina-grande-return-via-the-villafafila-lagoons-natural-reserve" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Salina Grande — return via the Villafáfila Lagoons Natural Reserve&lt;/a&gt;, which allows you to observe wildlife around the reserve's largest lagoon. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1306735956/camino-sanabres-and-moreruela-monastery-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino Sanabrés and Moreruela Monastery Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through historical sites and the Esla River valley.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of hiking trails in Villafáfila?
A: The trails in Villafáfila cater to various fitness levels due to the gentle topography. Most routes have minimal elevation changes. Out of the 8 available routes, 1 is easy, 6 are moderate, and 1 is considered difficult, offering options for everyone from casual strollers to more experienced hikers.

Q: Can I hike with my dog in Villafáfila?
A: While the region is generally welcoming to outdoor activities, specific regulations for dogs within the Lagunas de Villafáfila Nature Reserve may apply, especially concerning wildlife protection. It's advisable to keep dogs on a leash and check local signage or contact the Visitor Center for the most current guidelines to ensure a responsible visit.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in Villafáfila?
A: Yes, the gentle topography and well-maintained paths make many routes suitable for families. The easy trails, often focusing on birdwatching or short loops around the lagoons, are particularly good for children. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025267178/cistercian-monastery-of-santa-maria-from-granja-de-moreruela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cistercian Monastery of Santa Maria from Granja de Moruela&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 4.8-mile path that could be a good family option.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Villafáfila?
A: The hiking routes in Villafáfila are well-regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 3.4 stars from 5 reviews. Hikers often praise the unique natural environment, the abundance of birdlife, and the accessible terrain that makes for pleasant walks.

Q: Are there any specific viewpoints or natural features I should look out for?
A: Beyond the saline lagoons themselves, keep an eye out for the traditional adobe architecture, particularly the unique dovecotes scattered across the rural landscape. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5438069" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Otero de Sariegos Bird Observatory&lt;/a&gt; offers excellent views over the wetlands and its inhabitants. The vast, open steppe landscape provides expansive horizons, especially at sunrise or sunset.

Q: Is there parking available near the trailheads in Villafáfila?
A: While specific parking details for every trailhead are not provided, the region is generally accessible, and parking is typically available in or near the villages that serve as starting points for routes. For instance, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1123806437/laguna-salina-grande-from-villarrin-de-campos-circular-through-reserva-natural-de-las-lagunas-de-villafafila" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna Salina Grande from Villarrín de Campos&lt;/a&gt; would likely have parking options in Villarrín de Campos.

Q: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see besides birds?
A: While birds are the primary draw, the steppe and wetland environment supports other wildlife. You might encounter various small mammals, reptiles, and amphibians adapted to this unique ecosystem. The diverse flora also contributes to the natural beauty of the reserve.
tips: A bird and wildlife sanctuary that is part of the Lagunas de Villafáfila Nature Reserve, Laguna de San Pedro is a beautiful place to visit in spring after the rainy season.
You can find Villarrín de Campos at the southern end of the Lagunas de Villafáfila Nature Reserve. There is a nice park next to the Parroquia de Nuestra Señora de la Asunción where you can rest.

Otero de Sariegos is an uninhabited village located in the municipality of Villafáfila, in the province of Zamora, Castile and León. This place is known for its natural lagoon, which attracts numerous tourists and birdwatchers due to the diversity of species found there.

Historically, Otero de Sariegos has been linked to salt production since prehistoric times. The name "Sariegos" is believed to come from "saliegos," referring to salt producers. During the Middle Ages, salt exploitation reached its peak, and the area was filled with small villages dedicated to this activity.

Although currently uninhabited, the site remains of interest for its historical and natural value.
